Title: Genetic analysis of pigmented tuber flesh in potato

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Potential human health-promoting antioxidant pigment; nutritional quality trait in pigmented potato tuber flesh
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate locus associated with degree of tuber flesh pigmentation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A CAPS marker for potato chi mapped under the chromosome 5 QTL in parent 10618-01 affecting extent of tuber flesh pigmentation. 2. chi was identified as a promising candidate gene for the chromosome 5 QTL. 3. No direct functional proof was provided; the authors proposed possible promoter-region functional variation affecting tissue range of expression.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Diploid mapping population from 10618-01 × 320-02; association panel of tetraploid clones/cultivars
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: QTL co-localization with CAPS marker; linkage mapping; phenotyping of tuber flesh pigmentation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study did not conclusively establish chi as the causal gene; no association with flesh color was observed for the chi CAPS marker in the diverse tetraploid panel.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential candidate for marker-assisted manipulation of pigmented tuber flesh, but evidence is insufficient compared with Stan1.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Potential human health-promoting antioxidant pigment; nutritional quality trait in pigmented potato tuber flesh
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes toward increased extent of anthocyanin-pigmented tuber flesh but is not sufficient alone
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Stan1, the potato homolog of Petunia an1, co-localized with the chromosome 9 QTL for tuber flesh pigmentation. 2. The Stan1 marker explained 11% of phenotypic variation, more than the nearby AFLP marker E32M48-233 (8.1%). 3. In a diverse panel, all 21 clones with red or purple flesh carried an approximately 980 bp Stan1 CAPS marker allele, whereas only 21 of 53 white/yellow-fleshed clones carried it. 4. The data suggest a common Stan1-linked bHLH allele contributes to pigmented tuber flesh, although it is clearly not sufficient by itself.
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Diploid mapping population from 10618-01 × 320-02; association panel including pigmented and white/yellow tetraploid clones/cultivars
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: QTL co-localization with CAPS marker; linkage mapping; association analysis in diverse germplasm panel
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Likely transcriptional activation of anthocyanin biosynthesis in a tissue-specific manner
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin biosynthesis and tissue-specific anthocyanin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller contributing to tissue-specific anthocyanin accumulation in tuber flesh
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Stan1 is a homolog of Petunia an1. The study supports Stan1 or a tightly linked gene as associated with flesh pigmentation. Presence of the marker allele in some white/yellow clones shows the allele is not sufficient alone.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Implied role in tissue-specific expression in tuber flesh
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely part of anthocyanin regulatory complex, but not experimentally tested here
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Stan1-based markers may be useful for marker-assisted breeding to manipulate nutritionally important pigmented tuber flesh in potato breeding programs.
